file_name: Asare et al. - 2025 - Application of medical images for melanoma detection using a multi‐architecture convolutional neural.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning techniques, specifically multi-architecture convolutional neural networks (EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3) combined via a cascading technique, to detect melanoma from medical images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' and presents original experimental work. It details the development and evaluation of a multi-architecture convolutional neural network for melanoma detection using the ISIC-2020 dataset, reporting specific performance metrics (e.g., 94.86% accuracy) and comparing model architectures, which constitutes original research rather than a literature review.

title: Application of Medical Images for Melanoma Detection Using a Multi-Architecture Convolutional Neural Network From a Deep Learning Approach
brief_summary: This study addresses the critical need for non-invasive melanoma detection by evaluating deep learning models on the ISIC-2020 dataset. The researchers assessed three individual architectures—EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3—along with a novel cascading multi-architecture model that combines their layer outputs. EfficientNetB3 performed best among the single models, achieving 90.7% accuracy and a 97% AUC. However, the cascading multi-architecture model demonstrated superior performance, reaching an accuracy of 94.86%. These results indicate that the multi-architecture approach is an optimal and effective method for automated melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study's detection task using the ISIC-2020 dataset. The paper focuses on distinguishing melanoma from other skin lesions, specifically framing the problem as melanoma detection.

The ISIC-2020 dataset used in the experiments contains both melanoma and non-melanoma classes. The study treats the classification as a binary task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma/benign) implicitly by focusing on melanoma detection accuracy. The paper mentions 'benign and malignant' in the context of general literature review (e.g., Esteva et al., Kose et al., Wu et al., Wang et al., Codella et al., Li et al.) but the specific experiment in this paper targets melanoma detection.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to detect melanoma from medical images using the ISIC-2020 dataset. The target classes are 'Melanoma' and 'Benign' (non-melanoma). The study evaluates models (EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, and a multi-architecture model) based on classification metrics such as accuracy, AUC, F1-score, precision, and recall.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2020 Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions from over 2000 patients, sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC).

use.training: Primary dataset for training the EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3 models, and the cascading multi-architecture model.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; data augmentation was applied to the training data.
use.testing: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; performance metrics (accuracy, AUC) were reported on the dataset used for evaluation.
use.other: 913 melanoma images were added from ISIC 2019 to address class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported; the paper did not report p-values or significance tests.

EfficientNetB3.notes: Best performing single architecture. Achieved 92% accuracy in identifying malignant lesions and 89% in identifying benign lesions.

MobileNetV2.notes: Achieved 86% accuracy in identifying malignant lesions and 94% in identifying benign lesions.

InceptionV3.notes: Achieved 86% accuracy in identifying malignant lesions and 91% in identifying benign lesions.

Multi-architecture model.notes: Developed using cascading combination technique. Outperformed individual models.

ISIC-2020.notes: The study compared three individual CNN architectures (EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) and a proposed multi-architecture model. EfficientNetB3 was selected as the best single architecture for deployment. The multi-architecture model achieved the highest accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-architecture Convolutional Neural Network (cascading combination of EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3)
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models (EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3) were utilized; specific pretraining dataset not explicitly stated in the provided context for these specific models, though ImageNet is standard for such architectures.

architecture_details.combination_method: Cascading combination technique combining all layers (multiple layers) output of the models in a structured pipeline

backbone_details.EfficientNetB3: Compound scaling of network depth, width, and resolution
backbone_details.MobileNetV2: Depthwise separable convolutions (implied by architecture name, details not explicitly in text)
backbone_details.InceptionV3: Parallel convolution branches concatenated to form rich feature representation

classification_or_output_head.final_activation: Not explicitly stated, implied sigmoid/softmax for binary classification

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 224x224 pixels using Python's OpenCV
Pixel value scaling to the range of 0–1

Data augmentation techniques including rotation (up to 25 degrees), horizontal flipping, horizontal and vertical shifts (up to 20%), shear (0.2 angle), and random zoom (range 0.2) with 'nearest' fill mode
Removal of irrelevant features or artifacts to prevent interference with model accuracy

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Pixel values scaled to the range of 0–1 for computational efficiency.
Rotations of up to 25 degrees applied to handle different object orientations.
Applied to create mirrored images for improved generalization.
Shifts up to 20% of image dimensions to introduce positional variations.
Shear angle of 0.2 used to simulate non-uniform deformations.
Zoom range of 0.2 applied to allow for scale variations.
Applied as part of the augmentation techniques using Keras ImageDataGenerator.
Nearest fill mode used to maintain pixel structure during transformations.
913 melanoma images were added from the ISIC 2019 dataset to address class imbalance, resulting in 8480 augmented malignant images.

conclusions: The cascading multi-architecture model, which combines the layer outputs of EfficientNetB3, MobileNetV2, and InceptionV3, achieved the highest accuracy of 94.86% and an AUC of 97%, signifying it as the optimal architecture for melanoma detection among the tested models.

Among the individual architectures, EfficientNetB3 emerged as the standout performer with an accuracy of 90.7% and an AUC of 97%, outperforming MobileNetV2 (90.0% accuracy) and InceptionV3 (88.64% accuracy).

The multi-architecture approach improves upon individual model outputs by processing combined layer outputs in a structured pipeline, which reduces computational cost relative to performance gains and balances accuracy.

The study demonstrates that deep learning models can effectively detect melanoma from medical images, offering a non-invasive alternative to conventional biopsy-based methods which are invasive, time-consuming, and require specialized personnel.
The proposed multi-architecture framework provides a robust method for automated melanoma detection that can enhance early diagnosis and improve patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study addresses significant class imbalance in the ISIC-2020 dataset by adding 913 melanoma images from ISIC 2019, resulting in a balanced training set of 5936 malignant and 5936 benign images per split.

The multi-architecture model achieves high accuracy (94.86%) but at a substantially higher computational cost (40.57 GFLOPs) compared to individual models like MobileNetV2 (4.19 GFLOPs) and EfficientNetB3 (5.281 GFLOPs).
The paper does not report statistical significance testing (p-values) for the performance differences between the proposed multi-architecture model and the baseline single architectures.

External validation is limited; the study relies solely on the ISIC-2020 dataset and does not report performance on independent external cohorts to assess generalizability across different populations or imaging devices.

The authors acknowledge that deep learning models may suffer from bias in algorithmic decision-making and require large, diverse datasets, noting that further validation in diverse populations and clinical settings is needed.

The study compares the proposed method against other deep learning approaches but does not include a direct comparative benchmark against human dermatologists within the experimental results section, although it cites literature where DL outperformed dermatologists.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by the use of a defined train/validation/test split (11872/2544/2544) and data augmentation, but the specific protocol for preventing information leakage between splits is not detailed beyond standard augmentation practices.
